text: Hello everyone and welcome to the part three of total mesometrial resection. In this part we are going to demonstrate and explain the entire procedure for performing a parametrectomy.

Remember, exposure and removal of the parametrium is a key part for a patient who is undergoing a radical hysterectomy because parametrium is going to be forming the margin, especially the lateral margin for any form of tumor in the cervix or in the uterus which has spread onto the cervix.

It is very important to note that approximately two to three centimeters of the parametrium has to be removed so that it forms a sufficient margin because predominantly the lymphovascular invasion, if at all it occurs in the initial stages, it does not go beyond the two and a half to three centimeter mark.
The surgeon uses a Bissinger bipolar and a laparoscopic endosharer to perform the surgical procedure, demonstrating the use of these instruments in the hysterectomy.

The plane of dissection is more important than the instrument used for dissection and as long as the viewer is able to meticulously understand the plane of dissection, the entire surgical proceedings will proceed exceedingly smoothly.
The surgeon exposes the uterine artery at its origin and performs coagulation. Traction is applied to the ureter to ensure it is positioned away from the uterine artery.

This traction onto the ureter should be done in all the cases even if the uterine artery at origin is being performed for any other surgery in order to ensure protection from thermal damage to the ureter.
The surgeon coagulates or clips the right uterine artery at its origin, using either method based on surgical preference.
The surgeon demonstrates the dissection of the pararectal and paravesical spaces, using gentle grasping techniques on the ureter to ensure its protection during the procedure.
The surgeon applies gentle traction to perform ureterolysis, dissecting and mobilizing the ureter laterally away from the paracervical tissues.
The surgeon continues to mobilize the ureter away from the paracalpos, ensuring that the margins of the paracalpos are clearly visible and accessible for further surgical steps.
The surgeon coagulates the superficial uterine vein following the coagulation of the uterine artery, ensuring clear visualization of the vein's location and structure.
The surgeon coagulates and cuts the vascular parametrium, ensuring its liberation. Following this, the surgical field is prepared to expose the obturator nerve.
The surgeon exposes the obturator nerve, ensuring it remains visible and safely preserved during the procedure.

The surgeon performs further coagulation of the vascular parametrium, demonstrating the liberation of this structure. Care is taken to avoid thermal damage to the inferior hypogastric plexus when working deep within the parametrium.

The inferior hypogastric plexus should be always preserved and once it is preserved, it ensures a smooth bowel and bladder function as well as a sexual function at the end of the surgical proceedings.
The surgeon removes the deep parametrium, a crucial step in surgeries for bulky tumors such as TMMR, with visible tissue manipulation and separation from surrounding structures.
As one can see the entire surgery is getting proceeded without any uterine manipulator and as a result of this providing adequate strong and sturdy and constant all four words are important.

The surgeon applies traction to remove the anterior parametrium, which is located close to the bladder and contains nerve supply to the bladder. This removal aims to achieve a free margin and potentially reduce recurrence rates.
The surgeon carefully dissects the anterior parametrium, which is challenging due to the hazy margins near the bladder. The fibro-fatty tissue is expressed and removed during the parametrectomy.
The video shows the ureteric tunnel, visually resembling a circle within a circle, where the inner circle represents the ureter and the outer circle consists of parametrium tissue.
The surgeon identifies and dissects the space between the ureter and the adjacent tunnel, revealing the deep uterine vein located underneath the tunnel.
The surgeon safely coagulates or clips structures at the ureteric tunnel, demonstrating a technique often used by experienced surgeons to prevent ureteric fistula formation.

However, the technique of using the laparoscopic shearer as is being demonstrated can be safely replicated. In our experience of more than hundreds of cases of radical hysterectomy, we have had only one patient with a uretic fistula.

You can see that our predominant technique revolves around traction and dissection and very limited but very specific coagulation. If you observe the video three or four times, you will realize that our predominance is on the traction and on the dissection.

The surgeon has successfully dissected the anterior parametrium, leaving minimal tissue requiring coagulation, while ensuring vital structures such as the bladder and ureter are kept clear of the coagulation area.
The surgeon carefully de-roofs the ureter, exposing one or two veins in the area that require cautious coagulation to prevent bleeding.

The surgeon holds the parametrium under traction while performing dissection, ensuring safe passage of the ureter from the paracorpus. The circular layer of parametrium surrounding the ureter is completely removed, freeing the ureter.

This technique is important to learn. This technique is important to understand because once you understand the concept of the parametrium, doing a parametrectomy or having a paramaterial free margin with the dissection of the uretic tunnel is going to become exceedingly easy.

It is a conceptual surgery which one needs to master rather than following simply the steps of the surgery. I think one of the most important principles in any form of oncosurgery is to understand the principle of the surgery which is exactly what is being demonstrated here.
The surgeon continues to dissect around the ureter without using bipolar coagulation or shearer, demonstrating careful tissue manipulation to avoid devascularization.

The surgeon liberates fibrofatty tissue to establish a sufficient paramaterial margin, ensuring precise dissection without uterine manipulation. The fibrofatty tissue is carefully separated from surrounding structures, maintaining oncological principles.

The assistant provides precise traction, revealing the grossly liberated parametrium. A thin band of parametrium remains, which is easily removed, exposing the ureter that has been de-roofed from the surrounding parametrium.

The surgeon completes the de-roofing of the ureter from the anterior aspect of the parametrium, using traction and dissection techniques. The parametrium is fully liberated, and the ureter is visibly free from the paracolpos, providing a clear margin for dissection.
The surgeon displays the excised parametrium, measuring approximately three and a half to four centimeters, highlighting the substantial free margin achieved during the procedure.

The surgeon performs dissection at the bladder pillar where the ureter enters the bladder, facilitated by the prior complete parametrectomy. The surgical field is prepared for the upcoming therapeutic lymphadenectomy as part of the TMMR procedure.
I hope all of you are enjoying the video series which we are demonstrating in parts for the mesometrial resection.
